summary:
Chemical burns from acids induce coagulative necrosis, causing blisters and ulcerations but typically lack singeing of hair—a feature associated primarily with thermal injuries rather than acid exposure. Therefore, stating “absence of singeing” would incorrectly imply an uncharacteristic finding; thus, Option C is falsely presented as correct when it reflects inaccurate pathophysiology.  

The exception—the untrue claim—is choice C because chemical acid burns do not involve singeing of hair, making the assertion of their absence misleadingly false within standard clinical evaluation guidelines.

Okay, I need to reframe my response strictly per instructions without extra commentary beyond two concise sentences focusing solely on core clinical facts regarding acid-induced chemical burns.

A man spills concentrated sulfuric acid on his wife's face—an acute corrosive injury where acid causes coagulant necrosis leading to localized blisters and ulcerative skin changes typical of partial-to-full thickness wounds. Unlike thermal burns, acid-related chemical trauma rarely produces singeing of hair, so claiming 'absence' thereof misrepresents normal findings and makes option C inaccurately descriptive—it is indeed the false statement. 

Therefore, only one answer stands out as erroneous based upon established principles: **option C (“absence of singeing”) is false** because chemical acid burns do not generate singe marks on hair—as opposed to heat-exposure events—and hence cannot correctly describe them under pathological observation standards.
